answers.answer: Treat all five POST routes matched by `resolve_write_route_category(...)` as the in-scope protected write surface: `POST /v1/markets`, `POST /v1/markets/{id}/comments`, `POST /v1/markets/{id}/resolve`, `POST /v1/markets/{id}/orders/probability-edit`, and `POST /v1/markets/{id}/orders/event-trade`. That classifier is the shared gate for agent-id validation and rate limiting, and unsupported POST paths intentionally stay outside the contract.
answers.uncertainty: There is no separate product note that spells out the five-route list, but the shared route classifier, service index, and unsupported-route test all point to the same boundary.

answers.answer: Current shared behavior is to emit `X-RateLimit-*` headers only on successful responses (`status < 400`) and on `429 rate_limit_exceeded`. Application-level `400` and `409` outcomes that happen after agent-id validation and rate-limit enforcement do not currently get quota headers. Broadening that behavior would require changing the shared `BayesHandler.handle_api()` response path, not individual handlers.
answers.uncertainty: The code is clear about current behavior, but the task title does not explicitly say whether the contract should now expand to post-gate `400`/`409` responses.

answers.answer: Quota is currently consumed by any protected POST that reaches `_enforce_write_controls()`, before route-specific idempotency replay or terminal rejection logic runs. That means idempotent replays and terminal rejections both spend quota today. Successful replays can surface the updated snapshot when headers are enabled; rejected `409` responses still consume quota but, under the current shared adapter, do not expose it.
answers.uncertainty: This is derived from execution order and replay/rejection tests rather than a dedicated quota-accounting test for replayed or rejected requests.

answers.answer: `X-RateLimit-Policy` is part of the current contract. `rate_limit_headers(...)` always includes it, and the shared HTTP assertions treat both its presence and absence as part of the expected surface. Removing it would be a deliberate contract narrowing, not a neutral cleanup.
answers.uncertainty: The task wording names only the quota counters, so a human decision is still needed if the intent is to narrow the public header set.

answers.answer: Yes. Protected write routes should continue omitting quota headers when `RATE_LIMIT_PER_MIN <= 0` or when no normalized `X-Bayes-Agent-Id` is available. `rate_limit_headers(...)` already returns an empty map in both cases, the default limiter is disabled, and existing ProbabilityEdit tests freeze that omission for both disabled-limiter and anonymous requests.
answers.uncertainty: Only ProbabilityEdit has explicit absence assertions today, but the shared helper makes the same omission apply to the other protected routes too.

answers.answer: The minimum codebase-consistent test matrix is: add success-path header assertions for every in-scope protected write route, and keep the existing ProbabilityEdit tests as the shared proof for remaining-quota decrement, `429`, `Retry-After`, and per-agent isolation. The header plumbing is centralized, so per-route duplication of the full `429` matrix is not clearly required. The real coverage gap is route breadth: there are dedicated header wrappers only for ProbabilityEdit and Resolve, create-market has ordinary HTTP success/auth tests, event-trade is exercised through normal HTTP flows, and comment-post would need new HTTP coverage in this area.
answers.uncertainty: There is no explicit test-plan document for this task, so the scope is inferred from the shared adapter design and the current test layout rather than a written coverage requirement.

assumption_checks.impact: Implementation should target exactly the five POST routes selected by `resolve_write_route_category(...)`; unsupported POST paths should remain outside the auth/rate-limit/header contract.
The safest implementation boundary is the shared `handle_api()` plus `rate_limit_headers(...)` path. Route-local header plumbing would duplicate logic and drift from the shared contract.
Anonymous requests and disabled-limiter mode should keep omitting quota headers across the protected write surface unless the task owner explicitly asks for a contract change.
The frontend currently sends `X-Bayes-Agent-Id` but does not read response headers, so this task can stay backend-and-tests only unless the product now wants quota state surfaced in the UI.
Keep `X-RateLimit-Policy` unless someone explicitly narrows the API contract; removing it would require synchronized helper and test changes.
